query_summary: Three-round search. Round 1 (original 32 papers): 10 query strings across 14 databases/repositories. Round 2 (expansion to 70 papers, April 2026): 25+ additional queries targeting: SO104/CINCA95, SO161/SPOC, SO181/TIPTEQ cruise-based searches; MGL1610 PICTURES and MGL1701 CEVICHE; ODP Leg 141 Chile Triple Junction pre-site surveys; gas hydrate/BSR studies along Chilean margin; subduction erosion and basal friction studies; outer-rise bending-fault studies; accretionary prism and trench sediment fill; turbidite records; review chapters in The Andes Springer volume 2006 and Evolution Chilean-Argentinean Andes 2018. Additional sources checked in Round 2: GeoScienceWorld, MDPI Energies/Geosciences, Geo-Marine Letters, Journal of the Geological Society, Journal of Geodynamics, USGS Publications, OceanRep GEOMAR, Tectonics (AGU), Marine and Petroleum Geology. Round 3 (expansion to 100 papers, April 2026): 30+ additional queries targeting under-explored angles: SO211/SO244/SO261/SO165 cruise searches; specific author searches (Ranero, von Huene, Flueh, Völker, Geersen, Bello-González, Maksymowicz); topic gaps including slope basin stratigraphy (Valparaíso, Arauco), subduction channel reflectivity, rupture segmentation linked to seismic structure, Chiloé/Aysén/southern fjord seismics, northern Chile forearc active tectonics; and journals Pure and Applied Geophysics, International Journal of Earth Sciences, Geo-Marine Letters, Nature Communications. New papers added in Round 3: 30 new entries covering Juan Fernández Ridge CONDOR seismic (von Huene 1997, Laursen 2002), south Chilean forearc active tectonics (Geersen 2011 Tectonics, 2018 Tectonics, 2018 EPSL, 2011 JGS slope failures), Arauco Basin seismic stratigraphy (Becerra 2013), thermal models from BSR (Völker 2011 JGR, Villar-Muñoz 2014), outer rise bending faults Maule (Moscoso 2012, Moscoso & Grevemeyer 2015), megathrust reflectivity Iquique (Ma 2022), deep metamorphic wedge (Maksymowicz 2021), north Chile OBH tomography (Husen 2000, Kopp 2004, Bloch 2014, Bello-González 2024), flat slab transition (Contreras-Reyes 2024 EPSL), shelf/slope morphology (Völker 2014 IJES), Aysén fjord seismo-turbidites (Wils 2020), Chile Trench depositional morphology (Thornburg & Kulm 1987), cold seeps central Chile (Klaucke 2012), CEVICHE underplating (Arnulf 2022), Chile Triple Junction outer rise seismic (Contreras-Reyes 2019 G3).
sources_checked: AGU Journals (JGR Solid Earth, Tectonics, Geochemistry Geophysics Geosystems, GRL)
Journal of South American Earth Sciences (Elsevier)
Springer / SpringerLink (The Andes: Frontiers in Earth Sciences, 2006; Evolution of Chilean-Argentinean Andes, 2018)
Journal of Geodynamics (Elsevier) — additional author queries

papers.title: Seismic Structure and Tectonics of the North-Central Chilean Subduction Zone Along the Copiapó Ridge From Amphibious Seismic Refraction Tomography and Local Seismicity
papers.abstract_snippet: Wide-angle seismic refraction and reflection data were recorded on 36 OBS/OBH and 10 seismic land stations along an approximately E-W oriented 445 km long transect that aligns with the Copiapó Ridge off Chile's coastline during R/V SONNE cruise SO297. The study investigates structural controls on the north-central Chilean subduction zone at ~27°S using amphibious seismic refraction tomography and local seismicity recorded by the deployed instruments.
papers.relevance_notes: Directly reports acquisition and tomographic processing of offshore seismic refraction data (OBS/OBH) along the Chilean margin; includes specific marine survey lines at ~27°S; amphibious experiment with marine and land stations

papers.title: Shallow structure of the Northern Chilean marine forearc between 19°S–21°S using multichannel seismic reflection and refraction data
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: The research combines high-resolution bathymetry and seismic data to examine the northern Chilean erosive convergent margin between 19°S and 21°S. The study reveals a regionally developed forearc basin containing previously recognized features, with sediment accumulation reaching approximately 2 km at its deepest point along the middle slope. The investigation proposes that subsidence from subduction erosion mechanisms explains the basin formation process.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses multichannel seismic reflection (MGL1610 cruise data) and refraction data specifically acquired offshore northern Chile; reports four trench-perpendicular marine seismic profiles with geographic coordinates

papers.title: Seismic images of the Northern Chilean subduction zone at 19°40′S, prior to the 2014 Iquique earthquake
papers.abstract_snippet: The Northern Chilean subduction zone is characterized by long-term subduction erosion with very little sediment input. Multichannel seismic data from CINCA95 are reprocessed using pre-stack depth migration to produce detailed depth images of structures down to approximately 16 km. A megasplay fault separating distinct tectonic units is identified along with varying reflectivity patterns along the plate interface.
papers.relevance_notes: Processes existing CINCA95 multichannel seismic reflection marine data offshore northern Chile; provides seismic images of subduction zone at 19°40′S with identified fault structures

papers.title: The reflection seismic survey of project TIPTEQ—the inventory of the Chilean subduction zone at 38.2° S
papers.abstract_snippet: This study presents findings from an active-source seismology experiment across the Chilean subduction zone at 38.2°S. The seismic data clearly show the subducted Nazca plate with varying reflectivity. The researchers interpret high reflectivity at the plate interface as evidence of a subduction channel with thickness variations of 2–5 km.
papers.relevance_notes: Core marine seismic reflection survey of project TIPTEQ; reports acquisition of reflection seismic lines across the subduction zone at 38.2°S with detailed structural imaging of the subducting Nazca plate

papers.title: Structure and tectonics of the central Chilean margin (31°–33°S): implications for subduction erosion and shallow crustal seismicity
papers.abstract_snippet: Using wide-angle and multichannel seismic data at 31°–33°S, the team identified large-scale crustal thinning and long-term subsidence of the submarine forearc caused by subduction erosion processes. A trenchward dipping normal scarp marks the upper-to-middle continental slope transition, with a prominent lateral velocity contrast that propagates deep into the continental crust defining a major lateral seismic discontinuity.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses both marine wide-angle refraction and multichannel seismic reflection data at the central Chilean margin; reports seismic profiles with coordinates in the Juan Fernández Ridge collision zone

papers.title: Deep lithospheric structures along the southern central Chile margin from wide-angle P-wave modelling
papers.abstract_snippet: The study investigates crustal and upper-mantle structures of the subduction zone in south central Chile by analyzing seismic data from three distinct profiles. Wide-angle P-wave modeling examines how the thermal state of the incoming oceanic Nazca Plate influences subduction zone geometry across different plate ages ranging from 3 to 14.5 million years old at the trench.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports wide-angle seismic refraction data from three marine profiles (SPOC/TIPTEQ cruises) along south-central Chile margin; covers the 36–43°S region with OBS/OBH deployments

papers.title: Lithospheric flexure modelling seaward of the Chile trench: implications for oceanic plate weakening in the Trench Outer Rise region
papers.abstract_snippet: The study investigates lithospheric bending patterns along the Chilean subduction zone where oceanic plates of varying ages (0–50 Ma) enter the trench. Elastic plate modeling with variable elastic thickness is used to predict outer rise topography. Results reveal a systematic reduction in elastic thickness of up to 50 per cent as plates approach the trench axis.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses existing seismic refraction data from the Chilean marine trench region to constrain elastic plate models; the study is modeling-focused but draws heavily on marine seismic datasets along the full Chilean coast

papers.title: Seismic analysis and distribution of a bottom-simulating reflector (BSR) in the Chilean margin offshore of Valdivia (40° S)
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: Multi-channel seismic reflection data collected offshore from Valdivia (40°S) were processed to examine structural characteristics and the presence of bottom-simulating reflectors. Seismic structure velocity was determined through 1-D forward modeling, comparing recorded seismograms with synthetic data using varying physical parameters. Results confirm the presence of gas hydrates above the BSR, with the reflector's spatial continuity appearing interrupted or irregular due to faulting.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports processing of multichannel seismic reflection data acquired offshore Valdivia (40°S); identifies BSR and gas hydrate distribution from marine seismic survey lines

papers.title: Shallow seismic investigations of the accretionary complex offshore Central Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Thrust ridges are accretionary structures often associated with local uplift along splay faults and cold seep activity. The researchers examined the velocity structure of a thrust ridge system at the transition zone between the accretionary prism and continental shelf in central Chile, using five seismic refraction lines with ocean bottom sensors.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses five marine seismic refraction lines with ocean bottom sensors offshore central Chile; directly reports shallow seismic structure of the accretionary prism with identified thrust ridges

papers.title: Structural variability at the active continental margin off southernmost Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Newly acquired seismic reflection data from offshore Southernmost Chile between 54°S and 57°S reveal the intricate tectonic setting of this active continental margin. This poorly studied region has long been challenging for researchers due to adverse weather patterns that have hindered data collection. Three primary tectonic domains are identified south of the Magellan Strait.
papers.relevance_notes: Presents newly acquired marine seismic reflection data from offshore southernmost Chile (54°–57°S); images three tectonic domains including oceanic Antarctic plate, accretionary wedge, and continental backstop

papers.title: Gas hydrate and free gas estimation from seismic analysis offshore Chiloé island (Chile)
papers.abstract_snippet: One seismic section offshore Chiloé Island was analyzed to better define the seismic character of the hydrate-bearing sediments. Velocity analysis was used to estimate the gas-phase concentration and relate it to geological features. The velocity model allowed recognition of two important layers that characterize hydrate- and free gas-bearing sediments above and below the BSR respectively.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes marine seismic reflection data offshore Chiloé Island (~42°S); published in Andean Geology (primary Chilean geoscience journal); reports BSR distribution and gas hydrate estimation from seismic line

papers.title: Reflection seismic images and amplitude ratio modelling of the Chilean subduction zone at 38.25°S
papers.abstract_snippet: Near-vertical reflection data are used to examine structural properties within the Chilean subduction zone where a magnitude 9.5 earthquake occurred in 1960. The study employed seismic reflection imaging techniques and the reflectivity method to model traveltimes and amplitude ratios. The analysis revealed that the continental crust contains highly reflective horizontal and dipping events, with the Lanalhue Fault Zone imaged at shallow depths for the first time.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses near-vertical reflection seismic data from TIPTEQ amphibious experiment at 38.25°S; images subduction zone structure and first seismic image of Lanalhue Fault Zone

papers.title: Structure and geodynamics of the post-collision zone between the Nazca–Antarctic spreading center and South America
papers.abstract_snippet: The Chile Triple Junction, where the Chile Ridge subducts beneath South America, is examined using detailed tomographic imaging of the upper lithospheric Antarctic–South America subduction zone. Results indicate that extensive trench sediment has been frontally accreted to the forearc forming a 70–80 km wide accretionary prism.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine seismic refraction and multichannel seismic data from the Chile Triple Junction (~46°S); provides tomographic image from an amphibious OBS experiment covering the ridge-trench collision zone

papers.title: Long-term array observation by ocean bottom seismometers at the Chile Triple Junction
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: Seafloor seismic observations were conducted twice in the vicinity of the Chile Triple Junction (CTJ) in order to investigate the crustal activities associated with the subduction of the hot ridge. The researchers analyzed data spanning two deployment periods (2009–2010 and 2019–2021), detecting over 2,100 local earthquakes and identifying significant seismic patterns along the Chile Ridge and Darwin Fracture Zone.
papers.relevance_notes: Two multi-year ocean bottom seismometer deployments at the Chile Triple Junction; reports passive seismic marine data with spatial coverage of the CTJ region offshore southern Chile

papers.title: Tectonic control on sediment accretion and subduction off south central Chile: Implications for coseismic rupture processes of the 1960 and 2010 megathrust earthquakes
papers.abstract_snippet: A compilation of published and new seismic refraction and multichannel seismic reflection data along the south central Chile margin (33°–46°S) was used to study the processes of sediment accretion and subduction and their implications on megathrust seismicity. Wide-angle seismic profiles and multichannel seismic reflection lines image the accretionary prism and subduction channel structures along multiple transects.
papers.relevance_notes: Presents compilation of marine seismic refraction (wide-angle OBS profiles) and multichannel seismic reflection data from multiple cruises along south-central Chile (33°–46°S); directly relevant to marine seismic line catalog

papers.title: Structure and tectonics of the erosional convergent margin off Antofagasta, north Chile (23°30′S)
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: Coincident wide-angle and near-vertical seismic profiling, combined with gravity data, were used to study the Antofagasta sector (23°30′S) of the north Chile convergent continental margin where subduction erosion has dominated the evolution since at least the Mesozoic. A 2-D velocity field of the overriding and subducting plates was obtained using joint refraction and reflection travel time tomography. Gravity and velocity models indicate the overriding plate is mainly made of arc-type igneous basement.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports wide-angle seismic refraction and near-vertical seismic reflection profiles acquired offshore Antofagasta (23°30′S) from CINCA experiment; foundational paper for northern Chile margin seismic structure

papers.title: Upper lithospheric structure of the subduction zone offshore of southern Arauco peninsula, Chile, at ~38°S
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: A joint interpretation of swath bathymetric, seismic refraction, wide-angle reflection, and multichannel seismic data was used to derive a detailed tomographic image of the Nazca–South America subduction zone system offshore southern Arauco peninsula at ~38°S. Data were acquired during RV Sonne cruises SO161 (SPOC) and SO181 (TIPTEQ) projects. Seismic reflection data revealed outer rise bending-related faults cutting at least 6 km into the uppermost mantle.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports amphibious marine seismic refraction and multichannel reflection data at 38°S from SPOC/TIPTEQ cruises; images the full subduction zone with outer rise bending faults and accretionary prism structure

papers.title: Amphibious Seismic Survey Images Plate Interface at 1960 Chile Earthquake
papers.abstract_snippet: The southern central Chilean margin at the site of the largest historically recorded earthquake (Valdivia 1960, Mw=9.5) was investigated using an offshore-onshore seismic survey conducted in 2001 during SPOC (Subduction Processes Off Chile). Seismic data examine the subduction zone lithospheric structure and margin evolution over the 36–40°S segment.
papers.relevance_notes: Documents the SPOC 2001 amphibious seismic survey (RV Sonne SO-161) that acquired marine seismic profiles over the 1960 Valdivia earthquake rupture zone; foundational survey for the south-central Chilean subduction zone

papers.title: Revealing the deep structure and rupture plane of the 2010 Maule, Chile earthquake (Mw = 8.8) using wide angle seismic data
papers.abstract_snippet: The February 27, 2010 Maule earthquake ruptured ~400 km of the Nazca–South America plate boundary. Seismic refraction data reveal the fore-arc structure and subduction zone characteristics of the rupture region. A ~40 km wide accretionary prism is identified and the backstop location marking the seismogenic zone's updip boundary is determined using marine seismic reflections.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine wide-angle seismic refraction data imaging the deep structure of the 2010 Maule earthquake rupture zone; constrains accretionary prism geometry and subduction plate boundary dip offshore central Chile

papers.title: Thick, strong sediment subduction along south-central Chile and its role in great earthquakes
papers.abstract_snippet: Using a seismic survey conducted in 2017 with a 15.15-km receiver array, shallow structures along south-central Chile are imaged. Relative to other sediment-dominated subduction zones where sediment is typically accreted at the toe, an unusually large percentage of the thick trench sediments are consistently subducted beneath the slope. The well-drained, strong sediments are linked to the occurrence of great earthquakes in this region.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports 2017 marine seismic reflection survey along ~1000 km of south-central Chile using a 15.15-km multi-channel streamer; directly reports marine acquisition parameters and seismic processing for 1960/2010 earthquake zones

papers.title: Subducting seamounts control interplate coupling and seismic rupture in the 2014 Iquique earthquake area
papers.abstract_snippet: Marine seismic reflection and swath bathymetric data indicate that structural variations in the subducting Nazca Plate control regional-scale plate-coupling variations and the limited extent of the 2014 Iquique earthquake. Several under-thrusting seamounts correlate with the southward and up-dip arrest of seismic rupture. Subducting seamounts also fractured the overriding plate reducing plate-coupling in the shallow subduction zone.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic reflection and bathymetric data from offshore northern Chile (Iquique region); documents seamount structures on the incoming Nazca plate and their effect on subduction zone coupling

papers.title: Abrupt change in the dip of the subducting plate beneath north Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Wide-angle seismic refraction and reflection data from land and sea, complemented by hypocentre data recorded during the 2007 Tocopilla aftershocks, document an abrupt change in the dip of the subducting plate from less than 10° to about 22° at about 22°S. The abrupt change occurs at a depth of approximately 20 km at approximately 60 km landward of the trench.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine and land wide-angle seismic refraction and reflection data to constrain the geometry of the subducting Nazca plate offshore north Chile at ~22°S; amphibious experiment with marine component

papers.title: Seismicity in the outer rise offshore southern Chile: Indication of fluid effects in crust and mantle
papers.abstract_snippet: Micro-earthquake activity was recorded by two temporary arrays of ocean bottom seismometers deployed on the outer rise offshore southern Chile on oceanic crust aged 14 and 6 million years. Each array captured approximately 10 locatable events daily, generating a catalogue complete for magnitudes above 1.2–1.5. The dataset exhibits a high ratio of small to large events with a large proportion forming clusters whose members show a high degree of waveform similarity.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports OBS passive seismic deployments on the Chile outer rise (incoming Nazca plate); provides seismicity catalog and structural interpretation of fluid-driven fracturing in oceanic crust offshore southern Chile

papers.title: Heterogeneous structure of the Northern Chile marine forearc and its implications for megathrust earthquakes
papers.abstract_snippet: A density-depth model along the Nazca-South America subduction margin from 18°S to 23°30'S is constrained with seismic reflection profiles, published velocity-depth tomographic models, and relocated seismicity. The findings reveal a heterogeneous structure for the Northern Chile marine forearc with latitudinal and longitudinal segmentation of continental wedge properties. Changes in density can be explained by changes in fracturing degree, which could have an important control on the Iquique earthquake rupture process.
papers.relevance_notes: Integrates marine seismic reflection profiles and velocity models from 18°–23.5°S offshore north Chile; density modeling constrained by published seismic data from the northern forearc

papers.title: A topographic depression on the subducting Nazca plate controls the April 1st 2014 M8.1 Iquique earthquake rupture in Northern Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Along the Northern Chilean active continental margin, the subducting Nazca plate is characterized by a rough sea floor topography that has been suggested to control the rupture behaviour of megathrust earthquakes. The research team examined structural controls on the 2014 Iquique earthquake, which ruptured only a portion of a seismic gap previously active in 1877. Through integrated geophysical and geodetic analysis, seafloor morphology of the subducting slab plays a critical role in earthquake rupture processes.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses reprocessed multichannel seismic data (CINCA 1995) offshore northern Chile to image a topographic depression on the subducting Nazca plate controlling Iquique earthquake rupture; marine seismic data central to interpretation

papers.title: Seismic structure and tectonics of the continental wedge overlying the source region of the Iquique Mw8.1 2014 earthquake
papers.abstract_snippet: A trench-perpendicular seismic reflection profile acquired across the zone of maximum slip of the 2014 Iquique earthquake reveals structural features controlling earthquake behavior. The study identifies how crustal velocity structure influenced both rupture propagation and aftershock distribution patterns in this subduction zone.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports a trench-perpendicular marine seismic reflection profile across the Iquique earthquake source region in northern Chile; images continental wedge structure with identified velocity contrasts

papers.title: Imaging the source region of recent megathrust earthquakes along the Chile subduction zone: A summary of results from recent experiments
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: The paper examines the largest documented earthquake, the Mw 9.5 Valdivia earthquake of 1960, and three subsequent major earthquakes occurring along the Chilean margin. The authors present findings from marine seismological surveys designed to understand how geological structures influenced rupture patterns, with emphasis on sediment distribution and basement topography characteristics.
papers.relevance_notes: Review and synthesis of multiple marine seismic reflection experiments along the Chilean subduction zone; covers MGL1610 (PICTURES), 2017 south-central Chile survey, and TIPTEQ-related data; full coast coverage

papers.title: Impact of the Iquique Ridge on structure and deformation of the north Chilean subduction zone
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: The study examines how the subduction of the Iquique Ridge affects the structure and morphology of the north Chilean convergent margin. Advanced seismic reflection data processing maps ridge-related structures beneath the marine forearc with high precision. Significant ridge-related topography is currently subducting south of 20.5°S.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses advanced marine seismic reflection data processing (MGL1610 cruise) to image the subducting Iquique Ridge and its impact on the north Chilean margin structure; reports specific seismic profiles with geographic coverage

papers.title: Tectono-sedimentary evolution of marine slope basins in the Chilean forearc (33°30′–36°50′S): Insights into their link with the subduction process
papers.abstract_snippet: Connections between plate convergence and forearc basin development along central Chile's margin (33°30′–36°50′S) are examined using seismic data. The study identifies asymmetric half-graben structures on the slope controlled by faults that govern differential uplift and subsidence patterns. Three primary seismic sequences—basal pre-kinematic, intermediate syn-kinematic, and upper post-kinematic units—characterize the sedimentary architecture.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine seismic reflection data to characterize forearc basin architecture offshore central Chile (33.5°–36.9°S); identifies structural controls from seismic stratigraphic analysis

papers.title: The geometry of the Chilean continental wedge: Tectonic segmentation of subduction processes off Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: The study examines the continental wedge geometry along the Chilean margin using high-resolution bathymetry and geophysical data. Intricate patterns in wedge geometry challenge conventional classification approaches for convergent margin tectonics. The research demonstrates a latitudinal segmentation of the Chilean offshore subduction zone characterized by varying basal friction and fluid pressure conditions.
papers.relevance_notes: Integrates marine seismic and bathymetric data to characterize continental wedge geometry along the full Chilean margin; latitudinal segmentation model based on seismic velocity and structural data from multiple marine surveys

papers.title: Seismic facies changes along a nearly continuous 24° latitudinal transect: the fjords of Chile and the northern Antarctic Peninsula
papers.abstract_snippet: High-resolution seismic reflection profiles and piston cores from bays, fjords, and straits of the inland passage of Chile and the northern Antarctic Peninsula region were used to examine changes in seismic facies along a nearly continuous 24° latitudinal transect. These climatic regimes are characterized by differences in seismic facies.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses high-resolution seismic reflection profiles from fjords and inland straits of southern Chile; while not open-ocean marine surveys, the seismic data cover Chilean coastal waters from ~41°S to ~65°S

papers.title: Crustal Structure of the Incoming Iquique Ridge Offshore Northern Chile
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: The crustal structure and thickness of the Iquique Ridge is determined using multiple coincident seismic imaging techniques, finding that it is characterized by significant crustal thickening relative to typical Nazca plate oceanic crust, with a maximum thickness of approximately 13 km. Multi-channel seismic reflection data show along-profile structural variation including an intermittent Moho reflector near the onset of crustal thickening. P wave velocity models are derived from 2D tomographic inversions of travel time data from the 2016 PICTURES experiment.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic tomography (MGL1610 PICTURES cruise) targeting the Iquique Ridge on the incoming Nazca plate offshore northern Chile; both MCS reflection and wide-angle refraction data from the 2016 survey
papers.geographic_focus: North Chile (incoming Nazca plate, Iquique Ridge, ~20°S)

papers.title: Subduction erosion and basal friction along the sediment-starved convergent margin off Antofagasta, Chile
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: Prestack depth-migrated seismic records image slope debris that collects in a frontal prism, including approximately 30% pore fluid, which fills subducting grabens and is subsequently incorporated into an approximately 1.5-km-thick interplate reflective layer. Two mechanisms of basal erosion are identified from seismic images: erosion by seamount tunnelling and removal of large rock lenses of a distending upper plate. The study examines how subduction erosion is associated with strong interplate coupling and upper plate abrasion along the sediment-starved north Chile margin.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses prestack depth-migrated seismic reflection data from CINCA95 (SO104) offshore Antofagasta (~23°S); images subducting grabens, frontal prism and interplate layer from multichannel seismic profiles

papers.title: Subduction erosion along the North Chile margin
papers.abstract_snippet: Swath bathymetry off Antofagasta reveals tectonic features consistent with subduction erosion of the upper plate by the incoming oceanic Nazca plate. The North Chile margin lacks an accretionary wedge and is characterized by pervasive extensional faulting of the continental slope. The study examines the large-scale geometry and mass budget of the eroded Chilean margin using multichannel seismic data.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses multibeam bathymetry and multichannel seismic reflection data offshore north Chile to document subduction erosion; early foundational study for the CINCA campaign area

papers.title: Alteration of the subducting oceanic lithosphere at the southern central Chile trench–outer rise
papers.abstract_snippet: New geophysical evidence documents changes in the seismic structure of the incoming oceanic Nazca plate from the outer rise to just before its subduction at ~43°S in the deep-sea trench. Fast Pn velocities of >8.3 km/s were detected more than 200 km seaward of the outer rise, reflecting dry-peridotite composition. Towards the trench, uppermost mantle velocities become systematically slower (~7.8 km/s), interpreted as progressive serpentinization through bending-related fracturing.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic refraction and wide-angle reflection data from two profiles on the incoming Nazca plate offshore south-central Chile (~43°S); documents velocity changes indicating hydration of the oceanic lithosphere prior to subduction
papers.geographic_focus: South Chile (~43°S, Chiloé offshore, outer rise)

papers.title: Effect of trench-outer rise bending-related faulting on seismic Poisson's ratio and mantle anisotropy: a case study offshore of Southern Central Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Joint inversion of seismic refraction and wide-angle reflection data at the Southern Central Chile outer rise yields the P- and S-wave velocity structure of the incoming oceanic Nazca Plate. The Poisson's ratio in the uppermost mantle increases from ~1.75 in the outer rise to values of >1.8 at the trench axis, indicating increasing serpentinization. Bending-related faulting opens near-vertical cracks modifying porosity and permeability and providing pathways for seawater.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine wide-angle seismic refraction data (P- and S-wave) from the outer rise of southern central Chile; derives 2D Vp/Vs models from SPOC/TIPTEQ cruise data on the incoming Nazca plate

papers.title: Seismic structure of the north-central Chilean convergent margin: Subduction erosion of a paleomagmatic arc
papers.abstract_snippet: A joint refraction and reflection travel time tomographic approach derives a 2-D velocity-depth model for the north-central Chilean margin at ~31°S. The outermost fore-arc block is composed of volcanic rocks that are largely hydrofractured as a consequence of subduction erosion. The landward limit of this block coincides with a continental slope scarp suggesting tectonic subsidence.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic refraction tomography with multichannel reflection data at ~31°S north-central Chile; derives 2D velocity model of the outer forearc and identifies a paleomagmatic arc undergoing subduction erosion

papers.title: Structure and Tectonics of the Chilean Convergent Margin from Wide-Angle Seismic Studies: A Review
papers.journal: The Evolution of the Chilean-Argentinean Andes (Springer Earth System Sciences)
papers.abstract_snippet: This review compiles published 2-D velocity-depth models along the Chilean margin (22°–48°S) from wide-angle seismic studies to examine the structure and tectonic processes governing this convergent margin. It discusses subduction erosion in the north and sediment accretion in the south, identifying latitudinal segmentation of forearc properties. Two prominent velocity transition zones characterize the marine forearc and control megathrust seismicity.
papers.relevance_notes: Comprehensive review of wide-angle seismic refraction results along the full Chilean convergent margin (22°–48°S); synthesizes OBS/OBH data from CINCA, SPOC, TIPTEQ and other cruises into a unified tectonic framework

papers.title: Episodic development of a convergent margin inferred from structures and processes along the southern Chile margin
papers.abstract_snippet: Seismic reflection data acquired across the southern coast of Chile image structures formed along the continental margin revealing an episodic history of accretion, non-accretion, and possibly erosion. Structures at the toe of the continental slope suggest frontal accretion of 0.75 to 1.75 km of trench fill. A small accretionary wedge 20–30 km wide abuts the truncated continental metamorphic basement extending seaward from beneath the shelf.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine multichannel seismic reflection data across the southern Chile margin near Isla Mocha (~38°–40°S); images accretionary wedge structure and plate boundary geometry from Conrad pre-site survey data

papers.title: Basal Accretion Along the South Central Chilean Margin and Its Relationship to Great Earthquakes
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: In 2017 seismic reflection data were acquired along approximately 1,000 km of the south-central Chile margin using the R/V Langseth's 15 km long receiver array and 108.2 liter seismic source. A thick underthrust sediment layer separated from downgoing oceanic crust is proposed to be the source of strong coupling producing the world's largest earthquakes. Basal accretion of crustal slices from the subducting Nazca plate forms anomalous high-velocity wedge bodies at depth.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports 2017 MGL1701 CEVICHE cruise marine seismic reflection data along ~1000 km of south-central Chile (30°–44°S); images basal accretion and underthrust sediment layer from 15-km-long streamer profiles
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (30°–44°S, CEVICHE lines)

papers.title: Anatomy of a megathrust: The 2010 M8.8 Maule, Chile earthquake rupture zone imaged using seismic tomography
papers.abstract_snippet: Aftershocks from onshore seismic stations and ocean bottom seismometers are manually analysed to derive a 3-D velocity model of the 2010 Maule earthquake rupture zone using local earthquake tomography. An east-dipping high P-wave velocity anomaly (>6.9 km/s) is interpreted as the subducting oceanic crust. A subducted topographic high at 25 km depth coincides with a strong forearc Bouguer gravity signal, interpreted as a former seamount on the Nazca slab.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses OBS and onshore seismic data to derive 3D P-wave tomography of the Maule megathrust rupture zone; marine OBS component provides offshore velocity structure and plate boundary geometry
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (34°–38°S, Maule earthquake rupture zone)

papers.title: Shear wave velocity and Poisson's ratio models across the southern Chile convergent margin at 38°15′S
papers.abstract_snippet: A shear (S) wave velocity model and a Poisson's ratio model are derived along a profile at 38°15′S across the Chilean convergent margin at the site of the 1960 Mw 9.5 Valdivia earthquake. In the upper part of the profile, controlled source data from explosive shots used in the TIPTEQ Seismic experiment were processed for S-wave arrivals. The results show that seismic velocities and Poisson's ratios in the continental crust are consistent with geological features inferred from other studies.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses active-source seismic data from the TIPTEQ NVR experiment (SO181) at 38.15°S to derive S-wave velocity model; includes wide-angle data from explosive sources at the 1960 Valdivia earthquake zone
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (38.15°S, TIPTEQ line at Valdivia earthquake zone)

papers.title: Seismic investigation of the continental margin off- and onshore Valparaiso, Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Seismic investigations of the continental margin off- and onshore Valparaiso, Chile were carried out during the CINCA project in 1995. Wide-angle reflection and refraction data were acquired using Ocean Bottom Hydrophones (OBH) and ocean-bottom seismometers along profiles across the margin in the Valparaiso region. The velocity structure reveals the geometry of the subducting Nazca plate and the overriding South American continental crust in the Juan Fernández Ridge collision zone.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports wide-angle marine seismic refraction and reflection data from CINCA95 (SO104) offshore Valparaiso (central Chile, ~32°S); foundational paper from the CINCA campaign imaging the Juan Fernández Ridge subduction zone

papers.title: 3D simultaneous seismic refraction and reflection tomography of wide-angle data from the Central Chilean Margin
papers.abstract_snippet: Three-dimensional (3D) simultaneous seismic refraction and reflection tomography is applied to wide-angle data from the central Chilean margin, collected in 1995 by GEOMAR in a region where the Juan Fernández Ridge on the Nazca Plate is subducting beneath South America. The 3D velocity model reveals the large-scale margin structure and establishes the relationship between seamount subduction, plate deformation and seismicity patterns.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports 3D seismic refraction and reflection tomography from CINCA95 (SO104) wide-angle marine data offshore central Chile (~32°S); provides 3D velocity model of Juan Fernández Ridge collision zone
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (~32°S, Juan Fernández Ridge collision zone)

papers.title: Sedimentary fill of the Chile Trench (32–46°S): volumetric distribution and causal factors
papers.abstract_snippet: The total volume of the trench fill between 32 and 46°S is calculated to be 46,000 ± 500 km³. The volume calculation is based on a model geometry derived by interpolating between depth-converted seismic reflection profiles that cross the trench. The resulting latitudinal volume distribution is best explained by alternating glacial phases of high sediment flux from southern Chile and interglacial phases dominated by local factors.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses depth-converted seismic reflection profiles crossing the Chile Trench between 32°–46°S to calculate trench sediment volumetric distribution; marine seismic data directly constrains the trench geometry model
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (32°–46°S, Chile Trench)

papers.title: Turbiditic trench deposits at the South-Chilean active margin: A Pleistocene–Holocene record of climate and tectonics
papers.abstract_snippet: Sedimentary records from ODP Site 1232 and SONNE core 50SL in the Peru-Chile trench at ~40°S and ~38°S document frequent turbidites interpreted as seismically triggered paleoseismic indicators. During sea level highstands in the Holocene and MIS 5, recurrence times of turbidite layers are substantially higher, reflecting climate-induced reduction of sediment availability. Glacial turbidite recurrence times are within the same order of magnitude as earthquake recurrence times from historical records.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine sediment cores from the Chile Trench (~38°–40°S) complemented by seismic stratigraphy; while primarily a sedimentological study, it uses ODP and SONNE seismic stratigraphy as a framework for the trench

papers.title: Glacial-interglacial trench supply variation, spreading-ridge subduction, and feedback controls on the Andean margin development at the Chile triple junction area (45–48°S)
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: EM12 bathymetry and seismic reflection data were collected during the Chile Triple Junction cruise (March–April 1997) offshore 45–48°S, where an active spreading ridge is being subducted beneath the Andean continental margin. The Andean continental margin and Chile trench show strong segmentation reflecting the slab geometry and CTJ migration history. The study reveals a margin development shaped by processes spanning from subduction-erosion to subduction-accretion.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports bathymetry and seismic reflection data collected during a 1997 cruise in the Chile Triple Junction area (45°–48°S); images the accretionary prism, trench morphology and segment boundaries controlled by spreading ridge subduction

papers.title: Reestablishment of an accretionary prism after the subduction of a spreading ridge — constraints by a geometric model for the Golfo de Penas, Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Seismic studies offshore southern Chile reveal a 70–80 km wide accretionary prism seaward of the Golfo de Penas (GPAP) where the Chile Ridge collided with the South American Plate between 3 and 6 Ma. A mass balance analysis based on a 2D model of accretionary wedge and trench geometry explains the relative importance of sedimentary fluxes and deformation front migration for wedge restoration after ridge subduction.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses seismic data from the post-collision zone near Golfo de Penas (~46°S) to constrain geometry of the re-establishing accretionary prism after Chile Ridge subduction; applies seismic velocity models from Maksymowicz et al. (2012)
papers.geographic_focus: South Chile (~46°S, Golfo de Penas post-collision zone)

papers.title: Sediment loading at the southern Chilean trench and its tectonic implications
papers.abstract_snippet: Seismic and bathymetric data acquired off south-central Chile were used to estimate representative flexural rigidities and study the downward deflection of the oceanic Nazca plate due to sediment loading. Flexural modeling shows that efficient sediment subduction preferentially occurs in weak oceanic lithosphere, whereas wide accretionary prisms form in rigid oceanic lithosphere. Results suggest a general increase of fluid pressure inside the continental wedge north of 33°S.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine seismic and bathymetric data from south-central Chile to constrain flexural rigidity and trench sediment loading; seismic profiles define trench geometry along the southern Chilean margin

papers.title: Analysis of submarine landsliding in the rupture area of the 27 February 2010 Maule earthquake, Central Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Comparison of bathymetric datasets compiled before and after the 2010 Mw 8.8 Maule earthquake offshore Central Chile proves that no new submarine landslides on a size scale detectable with hull-mounted bathymetric echosounders (features >1 km horizontal size) formed as a direct consequence of the ground shaking. Gravity coring around a pre-existing slide feature offshore Concepción documents retrogressive failures triggered by aftershocks.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses pre- and post-earthquake bathymetric and sediment core data offshore Central Chile (~36°–38°S) to assess submarine landsliding; study relies on comparative seismic and bathymetric datasets from the Maule earthquake rupture zone
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (36°–38°S, Maule rupture zone offshore)

papers.title: Thermal state and concentration of gas hydrate and free gas of Coyhaique, Chilean Margin (44°30′S)
papers.abstract_snippet: Detailed velocity analysis using pre-stack depth migration was performed on multichannel seismic reflection line RC2901-734 offshore Coyhaique, Chile (44°30′S). The velocity model shows a hydrate-bearing layer above the BSR with high velocity (1700–2200 m/s) and maximum thickness of 250 m, and a free gas-bearing layer below with low velocity (1250–1400 m/s). Geothermal gradient estimates range from 35 to 95 °C/km.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes multichannel seismic reflection data on line RC2901-734 offshore Coyhaique (44.5°S); quantifies gas hydrate and free gas concentrations from BSR-related velocity analysis on a named marine seismic line

papers.title: Variability of the bottom-simulating reflector (BSR) and its association with tectonic structures in the Chilean margin between Arauco Gulf (37°S) and Valdivia (40°S)
papers.abstract_snippet: Multichannel seismic reflection data recorded between Arauco Gulf (37°S) and Valdivia (40°S) were analyzed to characterize the variability of the BSR. The BSR is discontinuous and interrupted by submarine valleys, canyons, faults or fractures, with occurrence more common south of Mocha Island. Tectonic uplift and structural instability create missing or double BSRs in some areas, and higher BSR amplitudes are associated with thrust faults in the accretionary prism.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses multiple multichannel seismic reflection lines from the Chilean margin between 37°–40°S to map BSR distribution; analyses seismic data from SPOC and other cruise datasets offshore Arauco and Valdivia
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (37°–40°S, Arauco–Valdivia region)

papers.title: Gas Hydrate and Free Gas Concentrations in Two Sites inside the Chilean Margin (Itata and Valdivia Offshores)
papers.abstract_snippet: Two sectors in the Chilean margin—Itata (~37°S) and Valdivia (~40°S) offshores—were analysed using seismic data to characterize gas hydrate concentrations. Maximum hydrate concentration reaches 17% of total volume in both sectors. Strong lateral velocity variations show a maximum value in Valdivia offshore (2380 m/s above the BSR) and a minimum below in Itata offshore (1380 m/s below BSR). Geothermal gradients range from 32–87 °C/km in Itata and ~35 °C/km in Valdivia.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes seismic reflection data from two offshore Chilean margin sites (Itata ~37°S and Valdivia ~40°S); quantifies gas hydrate and free gas from BSR velocity analysis on marine seismic lines
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (37°–40°S, Itata and Valdivia offshores)

papers.title: High Gas Hydrate and Free Gas Concentrations: An Explanation for Seeps Offshore South Mocha Island
papers.abstract_snippet: High velocities (up to 1900 m/s) and low velocities (1260 m/s) associated with high gas hydrate (up to 20% of total volume) and free gas (up to 1.1% of total volume) concentrations were detected close to Mocha Island (~38°S) from seismic velocity analysis. A variable geothermal gradient (65–110 °C/km) was estimated. Gas hydrate dissociation processes are proposed as the main supply source for seeps near Mocha Island.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyses two marine seismic sections offshore South Mocha Island (~38°S) for gas hydrate and free gas quantification; derives seismic velocity models from BSR amplitude analysis

papers.title: Gas hydrate versus seabed morphology offshore Lebu (Chilean margin)
papers.abstract_snippet: Seabed morphology growth related to fluid migration offshore Lebu (~38°S) is analyzed through stable water isotopes, bathymetric data, and marine seismic reflection profiles. The BSR detected in the seismic data indicates the transition between gas hydrate and free gas zones. The orientation of the mud cone relief is associated with fault-fracture pathways for fluid migration, and isotope data document past hydrate melting events.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses marine seismic reflection data offshore Lebu (~38°S) to characterize BSR distribution and seabed morphology linked to gas hydrate systems; seismic lines show BSR and fluid migration pathways

papers.title: A first estimation of gas hydrates offshore Patagonia (Chile)
papers.abstract_snippet: The spatial distribution of gas hydrate in the accretionary prism and forearc sediments offshore western Patagonia (50°–57°S), landward of the Antarctica–South America plate boundary, is documented for the first time. BSR-derived heat flow values of 30–70 mW/m² are calculated. Average gas hydrate concentration is 3.4%–10% and average BSR thickness is ~290 m, with estimated methane of ~3.0 × 10¹³ m³.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes multichannel seismic reflection data from offshore Patagonia (50°–57°S) to map BSR distribution and estimate gas hydrate concentration; first systematic gas hydrate study for southern Chilean Patagonian margin

papers.title: Gas Hydrate Estimate in an Area of Deformation and High Heat Flow at the Chile Triple Junction
papers.abstract_snippet: Gas hydrate spatial distribution, concentration and geothermal gradients are estimated in the accretionary prism and forearc sediments offshore Taitao Peninsula (45.5°–47°S) near the Chile Triple Junction. Velocity analysis of seismic profile RC2901-751 indicates gas hydrate concentrations less than 10% of total rock volume and extremely high geothermal gradients. The large estimated methane volumes (7.21 × 10¹¹ m³ hydrate, 4.1 × 10¹⁰ m³ free gas) make this region a potential massive methane source.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes marine seismic reflection profile RC2901-751 offshore Taitao Peninsula near Chile Triple Junction (45.5°–47°S); quantifies gas hydrate concentration from velocity analysis in a tectonically active region
papers.geographic_focus: South Chile (45.5°–47°S, Chile Triple Junction / Taitao)

papers.title: Gas Hydrate System Offshore Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: The Chilean continental margin has been extensively investigated to characterize gas hydrate systems through seismic data analysis. A representative seismic line (RC2901-727) at ~36°S shows a strong and continuous BSR. High concentrations of hydrate ranging from 10% to 17% of total volume are recognized along the seismic lines. The BSR is not present where intensive deformations with faults and fractures dominate.
papers.relevance_notes: Reviews gas hydrate system offshore Chile using seismic reflection data from multiple lines along the Chilean margin; analyzes BSR characteristics and gas hydrate concentrations from named seismic profiles including RC2901-727
papers.geographic_focus: Full coast (covering multiple sites from ~36°S to ~47°S)

papers.title: The nature, distribution, and origin of gas hydrate in the Chile Triple Junction region
papers.abstract_snippet: A bottom simulating reflector (BSR) is regionally distributed throughout the Chile Triple Junction region. ODP Leg 141 downhole temperature and logging data suggest the seismic BSR is generated by low seismic velocities associated with a few percent free gas in a ~10 m thick zone just beneath the hydrate-bearing zone. Low total organic carbon contents argue against significant in situ biogenic methanogenesis.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses ODP Leg 141 seismic data and borehole logs from the Chile Triple Junction (~46°S) to characterize the BSR and gas hydrate system; integrates seismic reflection profiles with drilling results from the triple junction site survey

papers.title: Structural framework of the Chile margin at the Chile ridge collision zone
papers.journal: Proceedings of the Ocean Drilling Program, Initial Reports
papers.abstract_snippet: Three multichannel seismic reflection lines collected on board the Conrad as part of the site survey for ODP Leg 141 drilling examine the tectonic and structural framework of the Chile margin in the vicinity of the Chile Triple Junction. The lines image the final phases of spreading-ridge collision with the lower trench slope, and the structure where a spreading ridge has been subducted 2 km beneath the lower trench slope near the Taitao Fracture Zone.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports multichannel seismic reflection lines collected as pre-site survey for ODP Leg 141 offshore the Chile Triple Junction (~46°S); labeled survey lines (745, 751, 762) directly image the ridge-trench collision zone
papers.geographic_focus: South Chile (~46°S, Chile Triple Junction / Taitao Fracture Zone)

papers.title: Seismicity in the upper plate of the Northern Chilean offshore forearc: Evidence of splay fault south of the Mejillones Peninsula
papers.abstract_snippet: A 3D tomographic model of the upper plate was developed using arrival times from the 1995 CINCA seismic network aftershock catalog from the Antofagasta earthquake (Mw 8.1). Seismicity partially defined by a clear lineament between 22 and 5 km depth is interpreted as an active splay fault ~50 km long south of the Mejillones Peninsula. The splay fault zone shows decreased Vp and Vs velocities and increased Vp/Vs ratio suggesting fracturing and fluid saturation.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses CINCA95 seismic network data and multichannel seismic reflection data offshore Antofagasta (~23°S) to image a splay fault in the upper plate; combines active-source tomography with relocated seismicity

papers.title: Geologic controls on up-dip and along-strike propagation of slip during subduction zone earthquakes from a high-resolution seismic reflection survey across the northern limit of slip during the 2010 Mw 8.8 Maule earthquake, offshore Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: A grid of closely spaced, high-resolution multichannel seismic (MCS) reflection profiles (1500 km total) was acquired in May 2012 over the outer accretionary prism offshore central Chile (~34°–36°S) using a 48-channel hydrophone streamer. Structure of the deformation front changes abruptly from subduction of most incoming sediment south of the northern limit of Maule slip to nearly complete accretion immediately to the north, occurring over a distance of less than 10 km.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports 1500 km of high-resolution marine MCS reflection profiles acquired in May 2012 offshore central Chile (~34°–36°S); images the northern limit of the 2010 Maule earthquake slip zone with 48-channel streamer data

papers.title: Post-seismic response of the outer accretionary prism after the 2010 Maule earthquake, Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Ten ocean-bottom seismometers were deployed from May 2012 to March 2013 in the outer accretionary prism updip from the greatest slip of the 2010 Maule earthquake. Transient seafloor pressure changes were observed on absolute pressure gauges with polarity changes suggesting shallow source depths, and their duration of hours to days was shorter than most geodetic transients from onshore GPS networks.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses OBS and absolute pressure gauge data from 10 instruments deployed offshore central Chile (~35°S) to monitor post-seismic deformation; provides marine seismic and geodetic data from the outer accretionary prism

papers.title: Subduction erosion and basal accretion in the Central Chile subduction wedge inferred from local earthquake tomography
papers.journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
papers.abstract_snippet: Results from a temporary network of 39 seismic stations deployed April–November 2017 in Central Chile reveal 3D elastic wavespeed anomalies using local earthquake tomography. Wedge geometry with body wavespeed anomalies near the interplate contact below 30 km depth are interpreted as accretionary complexes formed by basal accretion of crustal slices. Latitudinal differences are attributed to eroded material from both plates that changes the basalt-eclogite transition depth.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses local earthquake tomography from a 39-station network in central Chile to image the subduction wedge structure; identifies basal accretion of crustal slices and subduction erosion from seismic velocity anomalies
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (30°–35°S, central Chile subduction wedge)

papers.title: Tectonic Processes along the Chile Convergent Margin
papers.journal: The Andes: Frontiers in Earth Sciences (Springer)
papers.abstract_snippet: The Chile convergent margin spans more than 3500 km providing a unique natural laboratory where variations in subduction properties can be studied along a single plate boundary. The chapter synthesizes multichannel seismic reflection data from CINCA, SPOC, TIPTEQ and other cruises, along with multibeam bathymetry, to characterize tectonic processes. Extreme climatic change from the Atacama Desert north to glacially influenced south produces dramatic variability in trench sedimentation.
papers.relevance_notes: Synthesizes marine seismic reflection and bathymetric data along the full Chilean convergent margin from multiple cruises (CINCA, SPOC, TIPTEQ); comprehensive overview of seismic survey results and tectonic segmentation

papers.title: Seismic Images of Accretive and Erosive Subduction Zones from the Chilean Margin
papers.journal: The Andes: Frontiers in Earth Sciences (Springer)
papers.abstract_snippet: Modern seismic imaging methods are used to study subduction processes at the South American convergent margin using reflection and wide-angle/refraction data from the SFB267 collaborative project. Two areas of differing character are investigated: an erosive margin in northern Chile (19°–26°S) and an accretionary margin in south-central Chile (36°–40°S). Three main transects provide an overall impression of the internal structure below the Chilean margin.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic reflection and wide-angle refraction data from both the erosive north Chilean margin (19°–26°S) and the accretionary south-central Chilean margin (36°–40°S); CINCA and SPOC cruise data

papers.title: Geophysical Signatures and Active Tectonics at the South-Central Chilean Margin
papers.journal: The Andes: Frontiers in Earth Sciences (Springer)
papers.abstract_snippet: The ISSA 2000 and SPOC 2001 onshore and offshore projects surveyed the Chilean margin between 36° and 40°S, including the location of the 1960 Valdivia earthquake. The chapter discusses geophysical and active-source seismic data acquired offshore and onshore in the south-central Chilean margin, including reflection and refraction profiles from RV Sonne cruise SO161.
papers.relevance_notes: Presents synthesis of marine seismic (SPOC SO161) and land seismic data from the south-central Chilean margin (36°–40°S); covers the 1960 Valdivia earthquake zone with multichannel seismic profiles

papers.title: Latitudinal Variation in Sedimentary Processes in the Peru-Chile Trench off Central Chile
papers.journal: The Andes: Frontiers in Earth Sciences (Springer)
papers.abstract_snippet: Sediment fill of the Chile Trench reflects latitudinal variations in climate and morphology of Chile as well as sediment transport processes. Within the trench, northward transport of sediment is described between 33°S and 42°S along a northward-dipping trench-parallel axial channel. The chapter uses multichannel seismic data and swath bathymetry to document trench fill stratigraphy and volume distribution.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses multichannel seismic reflection data and bathymetry to document latitudinal variations in Chile Trench sediment fill between 33°–42°S; seismic stratigraphy defines trench morphology and axial channel architecture

papers.title: Holocene sediments from the Southern Chile Trench: a record of active margin magmatism, tectonics and palaeoseismicity
papers.abstract_snippet: Sedimentology, petrography and provenance of Holocene sediments from the Southern Chile Trench (36°–47°S) are investigated using gravity core descriptions, physical property measurements and quantitative X-ray analysis. Recurrence rates of sandy turbidites in the trench fan sediments indicate a link to the palaeoseismic record, making the turbidites useful as paleo-megaearthquake indicators. Core locations are defined using multichannel seismic reflection profiles to identify channel morphology.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses gravity cores from the Southern Chile Trench (36°–47°S) located with multichannel seismic reflection profiles; seismic data used for site selection and stratigraphic framework but study is primarily sedimentological

papers.title: Analyses of bottom simulating reflections offshore Arauco and Coyhaique (Chile)
papers.abstract_snippet: Two seismic sections offshore Arauco (~37°S) and Coyhaique (~44°S) in the Chilean continental margin were analyzed to characterize hydrate-bearing sediments. Velocity analysis was used to estimate gas-phase concentrations and identify hydrate and free gas layers relative to the BSR. In the southern sector (Coyhaique), maximum gas hydrate and free gas concentrations were calculated at 15% and 2.7% of total volume respectively.
papers.relevance_notes: Analyzes two marine seismic reflection sections offshore Arauco (~37°S) and Coyhaique (~44°S) for BSR characterization and gas hydrate quantification; uses named Chilean margin seismic lines
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (37°S Arauco and 44°S Coyhaique offshore)

papers.title: Active tectonics of the South Chilean marine fore arc (35°S–40°S)
papers.abstract_snippet: A unique acoustic data set including >90% bathymetric coverage of the continental slope reveals an along-strike heterogeneity of the South Chilean marine fore arc (35°S–40°S). The South Chilean marine forearc is separated into four tectonic segments: Concepción North, Concepción South, Nahuelbuta, and Tolten (from north to south). Splay faults are seismically imaged in multiple segments, with normal faults and transpressional structures documenting active deformation. Giant submarine landslides observed offshore Arauco Peninsula coincide with the segment boundary between the 1960 and 2010 earthquake rupture zones.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses swath bathymetry combined with high- and low-frequency reflection seismic profiles and heat-flow measurements across the south Chilean margin forearc (35°–40°S); identifies four tectonic segments from marine seismic imaging
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (35°–40°S, Concepción–Arauco region)

papers.title: Active Tectonics of the North Chilean Marine Forearc and Adjacent Oceanic Nazca Plate
papers.abstract_snippet: New multibeam bathymetry data provide an unprecedented view of the tectonic regime and its along-strike heterogeneity of the North Chilean marine forearc and oceanic Nazca Plate between 19°S and 22.75°S. The tectonic map reveals a middle and upper slope configuration dominated by pervasive extensional faulting, with some faults outlining a >500-km-long ridge interpreted as remnants of a Jurassic magmatic arc. Lower slope deformation includes slope-failures, normal faulting, re-entrant embayments, and NW-SE trending anticlines and synclines resulting from subducting lower-plate topography and gravitational forearc collapse.
papers.relevance_notes: Combines multibeam bathymetry with subbottom profiler and legacy seismic reflection lines to derive a tectonic map of the north Chilean marine forearc (19°–22.75°S); uses published and previously unpublished marine seismic reflection data

papers.title: Does permanent extensional deformation in lower forearc slopes indicate shallow plate-boundary rupture?
papers.abstract_snippet: The paper documents permanent extensional deformation of the lower slope localized to the region 20.8°S–22°S along the North Chilean margin. Critical taper analysis, comparison of permanent deformation to inter-seismic seismicity and plate-coupling models, and observations from other subduction zones including the 2011 Tohoku-Oki area suggest that the normal faults at the lower slope may have resulted from shallow, possibly near-trench breaking earthquake ruptures. The study used multibeam bathymetry and legacy seismic reflection lines to define the deformation pattern.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses multibeam bathymetry and legacy marine seismic reflection lines offshore northern Chile (20.8°–22°S) to characterize extensional faulting in the lower forearc slope; interprets pattern in context of shallow plate-boundary rupture

papers.title: Seismic structure and tectonics of the southern Arauco Basin, south-central Chile (~38°S)
papers.abstract_snippet: The kinematic evolution of the Arauco Basin is studied using high-resolution seismic reflection data across the southern Arauco forearc basin. Three structural domains are identified: inversion, extension, and accretion. Seven syn-kinematic sequences are recognized overlying the Late Carboniferous to Triassic basement. Shortening began in the southern Arauco Basin around 34 Ma, coeval with a major readjustment of the plate convergence rate, and a marked erosional unconformity (34–23 Ma) represents subsequent uplift during the Oligocene, followed by a contractional Miocene deformation phase.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses high-resolution marine seismic reflection data to characterize the structural and stratigraphic architecture of the southern Arauco forearc basin (~38°S); directly reports marine seismic reflection profiles and their interpretation for basin evolution
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (~38°S, southern Arauco Basin offshore)

papers.title: Thermal control of the seismogenic zone of southern central Chile
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: Thermal models for the Chile subduction zone are developed along two profiles at 38.2°S and 42°S within the rupture area of the 1960 Valdivia earthquake and south of the 2010 Maule earthquake. The age difference of the subducting Nazca Plate has a major impact on the thermal regime. Seafloor heat flow values derived from the depth of gas hydrate bottom-simulating reflectors confirm that the southern area has higher heat flow due to the younger, hotter incoming plate. The seismogenic zone widens and shifts landward to greater depths from south to north.
papers.relevance_notes: Derives heat flow values from BSR depths on marine seismic reflection profiles at two south-central Chile transects (38.2°S and 42°S); uses BSR-constrained thermal models from SPOC and TIPTEQ cruise seismic data

papers.title: Morphology and geology of the continental shelf and upper slope of southern Central Chile (33°S–43°S)
papers.abstract_snippet: The geologic setting of the southern Central Chilean continental shelf (33°S–43°S) is summarized using recently published geophysical, seismological, sedimentological and bio-geochemical data, together with unpublished reflection seismic profiles, swath bathymetry and biological observations. Shelf basins show highly variable thickness of Oligocene–Quaternary sedimentary units dissected by marine continuations of upper plate faults known from land. A paleo-accretionary complex forms the bulk of the continental margin, sandwiched between the present active accretionary prism and the continental crust.
papers.relevance_notes: Integrates unpublished marine seismic reflection profiles with swath bathymetry to characterize the continental shelf and upper slope of southern Central Chile (33°–43°S); provides synthesis of shelf basin architecture and margin evolution using named seismic lines
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (33°–43°S, shelf and upper slope)

papers.title: Heat flow in the southern Chile forearc controlled by large-scale tectonic processes
papers.abstract_snippet: Between 33°S and 47°S, heat flow through the southern Chile forearc is estimated using the depth of the bottom-simulating reflector obtained from a comprehensive database of reflection seismic profiles. On the upper and middle continental slope, heat flow is 30–60 mW/m², whereas the actively deforming accretionary wedge on the lower slope shows heat flow reaching ~90 mW/m², indicating advecting pore fluids from deeper in the subduction zone. Large-scale tectonic features including the subducting Juan Fernández Ridge, oceanic fracture zones, and the Chile Ridge spreading center modulate the heat flow pattern.
papers.relevance_notes: Derives heat flow from BSR depth on a comprehensive database of marine seismic reflection profiles along the full southern Chile forearc (33°–47°S); reports latitudinal heat flow variations constrained by marine seismic data
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile / South Chile (33°–47°S, southern forearc)

papers.title: Outer rise seismicity related to the Maule, Chile 2010 mega-thrust earthquake and hydration of the incoming oceanic lithosphere
papers.abstract_snippet: During the first weeks following the 2010 Maule main shock, an outer rise seismic cluster of >450 events (mainly extensional, Mw=4–6) formed seaward of the main slip maximum. Outer rise seismicity is strongly correlated with the heterogeneous coseismic slip distribution. Seismic constraints reveal reduced P-wave velocities in the uppermost mantle at the trench-outer rise region (7.5–7.8 km/s), suggesting serpentinization. High-resolution bathymetry reveals bending-related faulting more intense seaward of maximum slip.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses seismic refraction wide-angle data and high-resolution bathymetry from the outer rise offshore Maule (~34°–36°S) to document bending-faulting and lithospheric hydration; combines OBS seismic data with aftershock catalog from the Chile Trench outer rise
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (~34°–36°S, outer rise of the Maule earthquake)

papers.title: Bending-related faulting of the incoming oceanic plate and its effect on lithospheric hydration and seismicity: A passive and active seismological study offshore Maule, Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: New active and passive seismological data offshore Maule, Chile, examine the dependency between incoming plate structure, bending-related faulting, lithospheric hydration, and outer rise seismic activity. A 2D Poisson's ratio distribution is derived from P- and S-wave seismic wide-angle data collected at the trench-outer rise. High values of Poisson's ratio in the uppermost mantle suggest that the oceanic lithosphere is highly hydrated due to water infiltration through bending-related normal faults outcropping at the seafloor between 34°S and 35°S.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports active-source OBS wide-angle P- and S-wave seismic data from the outer rise of the incoming Nazca plate offshore Maule (34°–35°S); derives 2D velocity and Poisson's ratio models from marine seismic wide-angle data
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (34°–35°S, outer rise of the Maule segment)

papers.title: Megathrust reflectivity reveals the updip limit of the 2014 Iquique earthquake rupture
papers.abstract_snippet: State-of-the-art seismic reflection data processing is used to investigate the tectonic setting associated with the apparent updip arrest of rupture propagation during the 2014 Mw 8.1 Iquique earthquake at 15 km depth. A spatial correspondence is documented between the rupture area and seismic reflectivity of the plate boundary. North and updip of the rupture area, a coherent, highly reflective plate boundary indicates excess fluid pressure that may prevent the accumulation of elastic strain. In contrast, the rupture area is characterized by the absence of plate boundary reflectivity, indicative of low fluid pressure and strain accumulation.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses advanced state-of-the-art processing of MGL1610 PICTURES marine seismic reflection data offshore northern Chile to image megathrust reflectivity at the 2014 Iquique earthquake updip limit; directly reports marine seismic processing results for plate boundary imaging
papers.geographic_focus: North Chile (19°–21°S, Iquique earthquake updip limit)

papers.title: Deep Structure of the Continental Plate in the South-Central Chilean Margin: Metamorphic Wedge and Implications for Megathrust Earthquakes
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: Interpretation of 2D seismic velocity models and analysis of seismic waves reflected in the deep zone of the continental crust are presented along the south-central Chilean subduction zone (36°S–41°S). This zone includes the southern portion of the Mw8.8 Maule earthquake (2010) and the northern patch of the Mw9.6 Valdivia earthquake (1960). A variation of seismic structure along the margin is interpreted in terms of the distribution of Permo-Triassic metamorphic units. The change in observed deep structural style suggests differences in frictional properties between the tectonic plates in the zones where the Maule and Valdivia earthquakes ruptured.
papers.relevance_notes: Presents 2D seismic velocity models from wide-angle OBS/OBH data and deep reflection seismic profiles along the south-central Chile margin (36°–41°S); identifies deep metamorphic wedge structure controlling megathrust earthquake segmentation

papers.title: Tectonic control of the subducting Juan Fernández Ridge on the Andean margin near Valparaiso, Chile
papers.abstract_snippet: Near the latitude of Valparaiso, Chile, fundamental changes in the Benioff Zone configuration, volcanic arc activity, and the structure of the continental margin occur opposite the subducting Juan Fernández Ridge. Upper plate tectonics related to subduction of the ridge were studied in the two-degree segment offshore Valparaiso, extending from the shelf edge seaward across the eastern end of Juan Fernández Ridge. The ridge uplifts the upper plate and sediments of the Valparaiso Basin are deformed against its southern flank. In the trench axis, the ridge forms a basement barrier behind which sediments 2.5 km deep have ponded.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports multichannel seismic reflection and wide-angle data from the CONDOR experiment offshore Valparaiso (~32°–33°S); foundational study of the Juan Fernández Ridge subduction zone structure using named marine seismic profiles
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (~32°–33°S, Valparaiso / Juan Fernández Ridge collision)

papers.title: Neotectonic deformation of the central Chile margin: Deepwater forearc basin formation in response to hot spot ridge and seamount subduction
papers.abstract_snippet: The Valparaiso Basin is a deep-water forearc basin underlying a 40×60 km mid-slope terrace at 2.5 km water depth on the central Chile margin. Seismic-reflection data from the CONDOR investigation image a 3–3.5 km thick sediment succession. In response to underthrusting of the Juan Fernández Ridge, the basin fill is deformed above seaward-vergent outer forearc compressional highs. Basal erosion enhanced by JFR subduction thinned the continental crust and increased the subsidence rate to form the Valparaíso Forearc Basin.
papers.relevance_notes: Reports marine seismic reflection data (CONDOR project) imaging the Valparaíso deep-water forearc basin at the Juan Fernández Ridge subduction zone (~32°S); characterizes basin stratigraphy, deformation history, and 3D geometry from seismic profiles

papers.title: Local earthquake tomography of shallow subduction in north Chile: A combined onshore and offshore study
papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
papers.abstract_snippet: Aftershock data from the great Antofagasta earthquake (Mw = 8.0) are inverted simultaneously for hypocenter locations and 3D VP and VP/VS structure using a dense 44-station seismic network including ocean bottom hydrophones. The subducted plate is imaged between 20 and 50 km depth as an eastward dipping high VP feature. High VP/VS ratios within the oceanic crust indicate elevated fluid content. The combined onshore and offshore approach constrains shallow subduction geometry and physical properties of the plate interface.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses 44-station seismic network including ocean bottom hydrophones (OBH) offshore Antofagasta, north Chile (~23°S) to derive 3D local earthquake tomography; marine component is integral to constraining shallow subduction geometry
papers.geographic_focus: North Chile (~23°S, Antofagasta / CINCA region offshore)

papers.title: High-resolution image of the North Chilean subduction zone: seismicity, reflectivity and fluids
papers.abstract_snippet: High-precision locations for 5,250 earthquakes are obtained in the Iquique segment of the northern Chilean subduction zone from two temporary local seismic networks around 21°S. A double seismic zone in the downgoing Nazca slab is clearly identified. A combined interpretation of seismicity and reflectivity along the seismic ANCORP'96 experiment suggests the prevalence of fluid processes in the subducted oceanic crust and uppermost 20 km of the mantle. Released fluids migrate upwards, explaining the enhanced seismic reflectivity in the uppermost mantle of the Nazca slab.
papers.relevance_notes: Combines local seismic network data with marine-based ANCORP'96 seismic reflection data offshore northern Chile (~21°S) to interpret fluid processes and reflectivity of the subduction zone; uses the ANCORP marine seismic profile as primary structural framework

papers.title: Sidescan sonar imagery of widespread fossil and active cold seeps along the central Chilean continental margin
papers.abstract_snippet: The central Chilean subduction zone between 35°S and 37°S was investigated to identify and document fluid flow and fluid venting within the forearc region using multibeam bathymetry, high-resolution sidescan sonar, chirp subbottom profiling and reflection seismic data. Surprisingly little evidence of fluid venting was found along the mid-slope region compared to other subduction zones, but abundant indications of active and predominantly fossil fluid venting occur along the upper slope between 36.5°S and 36.8°S at the seaward margin of an intraslope basin.
papers.relevance_notes: Uses sidescan sonar, chirp subbottom profiler and reflection seismic data offshore central Chile (35°–37°S) to map cold seep distribution; seismic data constrains structural context for fluid seepage at the continental slope
papers.geographic_focus: Central Chile (35°–37°S, continental slope offshore Biobío region)

papers.title: High-resolution seismic tomography of the transition zone from normal to flat slab subduction in central Chile: Implications for volcanoes, plate coupling and flat subduction
papers.abstract_snippet: High-resolution Vp, Vs and Vp/Vs models are determined in the central Chile subduction zone where normal slab subduction transits to flat slab subduction between latitudes 22°–31°S. The transition zone is bounded by the 1995 Antofagasta earthquake to the north and the 2015 Illapel earthquake to the south.
